Biomedical engineering · Drug delivery · Biomaterials

I study materials that can listen to biology.

I am Sanjoy Kumar Basu, a pharmaceutical scientist and biomedical engineer. In July 2026, I will begin my PhD at IIT Guwahati and explore how mechanics, fabrication and drug delivery can come together in useful biomedical systems.

About

I started with medicines, then moved to materials.

My first training was in pharmacy. I learned how a drug is put into a tablet, and how that changes the way it works in the body.

That pulled me toward the material side of things, so I moved into biomedical engineering. At IIT Guwahati I have worked with natural polymers, nanomaterials, sensors and small devices.

I like projects that stay close to a real problem. A microsphere for a drug. A film made from banana waste. A low cost health monitor for places far from a hospital. The habit is the same each time. Build it with care, test it honestly and keep the person who will use it in mind.

PhD direction

Starting July 2026

Designing biomaterials that sense force, carry treatment and take shape.

Cells are quiet listeners. They feel a push, a pull, the stiffness of whatever holds them, and they answer in ways we are only starting to read. Mechanobiology is the study of that quiet conversation. My work starts there, with force treated as a language.

From that point I look outward. Drug delivery asks how a treatment reaches the right place at the right moment. 3D printing asks if we can build soft living structure layer by layer, close to the feel of real tissue. Where these three meet there is a small unfinished space that some call mechanopharmaceutics. I do not know yet which exact problem I will pick. I do know the kind of question that keeps me up at night, and this is it.

Research

Projects I have worked on.

01

IIT Guwahati · 2025 to now · M.Tech dissertation

Biodegradable films for food packaging

I am making a film from natural polymers that can take the place of some plastic packaging. The base is nanocellulose pulled from banana pseudo stem. Carbon quantum dots go in too. Together they make the film stronger, block UV light, slow down spoiling and add some antibacterial action. The plan is simple. Turn farm waste into something useful.

NanocelluloseCarbon quantum dotsFTIRFESEMFETEMProbe sonication
02

IIT Guwahati · 2025 · Group project

AROGYAVITALS, a health monitor for rural areas

Our group built a small, low cost device for maternal health checks. It reads heart rate, temperature and blood pressure. The readings go over a wireless link and get stored, so a doctor can look at them from far away. We made it for places where a clinic is hard to reach.

Sensor interfacingFusion 3603D printingCircuit design
03

M.Pharm dissertation · 2023 to 2024

Floating microspheres of metformin

For my master's in pharmacy I made floating microspheres of metformin with permeation enhancers. The aim was to keep the drug in the stomach longer, control how it releases and lift how much the body can absorb. It also points to a way of handling other BCS Class III drugs.

Ionic gelationEx vivo gut sacUV-VisDissolution testing
04

B.Pharm · 2022 · Review

A review on microspheres

An early review where I compared the ways of making microspheres and the tests used to check them. It looked at things like particle size and release behaviour, and where these systems fit in controlled drug delivery.

Literature reviewRelease kineticsComparative analysis
At IIT Guwahati

Two years that changed how I think about research.

I joined in July 2024 for my M.Tech. The campus gave me space to bring my pharmacy background together with materials, biology and engineering. The same place will be the start of my PhD in 2026. Here are a few photos from around it.

Toolkit

What I can do in the lab.

Grouped by how much I have actually done.

Formulation and delivery

Hands on

Microspheres, floating systems, release and dissolution tests, ex vivo gut sac, biopolymer films.

Materials characterisation

Hands on

FTIR, XRD with Segal crystallinity, FESEM, FETEM, UV-Vis. Plus making nanocellulose and carbon quantum dots.

Cell and molecular biology

From coursework

Cell culture, passaging, viability checks, RNA and DNA extraction, cDNA, RT-qPCR.

Bioinformatics and computation

From coursework

NCBI, UniProt, PDB, BLAST, Clustal Omega, docking with CB-Dock, ProtParam.

Instruments I have used

Seen in use

Confocal and inverted microscopes, AFM, Raman, flow cytometry, TGA, DSC, rheometer, 3D bioprinter.

Industry training

Industry

Standard Pharmaceuticals, Kolkata, 2021. Dosage form making, QC and QA, documentation and basic regulatory work.

Outside the lab

Drawing, sport and a habit of travelling.

I drew a lot growing up and finished a few drawing courses. It taught me to slow down and look closely. I have played tug of war for my team twice and won gold once. When there is time I read story books, and I like to travel and see new places.
